overview: CCNA wireless coverage in this material focuses on architecture and management (SOHO vs. enterprise, WLC, CAPWAP) rather than 802.11 protocol/encryption detail, which the source notes is covered in an earlier, separate volume.
definitions: Autonomous AP: an access point that creates and fully controls its own WLAN independently, with no central controller (typical of SOHO).
CAPWAP: the protocol used to tunnel traffic between lightweight APs and a WLC in enterprise (SDA) architectures.
WLC (Wireless LAN Controller): the central device managing lightweight APs in an enterprise wireless deployment.
core_concepts: SOHO architecture: a single integrated device acts as router, switch, AP, and firewall; the AP is Autonomous and does not use CAPWAP because there is no WLC to tunnel to.
Enterprise/SDA architecture: lightweight APs tunnel client traffic to a WLC using CAPWAP; DNA Center can discover WLCs and display them on the topology map, unifying wired and wireless management.
protocol_operation: SOHO: Autonomous AP receives 802.11 frames from wireless clients and converts them directly to 802.3 Ethernet frames onto the wired network — no CAPWAP tunnel involved.
Enterprise: lightweight AP encapsulates client traffic in CAPWAP and forwards it to the WLC for processing/policy.
packet_flow: SOHO: Wireless client (802.11) -> Autonomous AP -> converted to 802.3 Ethernet -> wired LAN, no controller involved. Enterprise: Wireless client (802.11) -> Lightweight AP -> CAPWAP tunnel -> WLC -> policy applied -> wired network / DNA Center visibility.

troubleshooting.symptom: A newly installed AP in a small SOHO office is powered on, but devices can't see its SSID.
troubleshooting.cause: In SOHO architecture, an Autonomous AP does not use CAPWAP or depend on a WLC — the SSID issue is local to the AP's own standalone configuration/radio, not a controller-tunnel problem.
troubleshooting.fix: Verify the AP's own standalone SSID/radio configuration directly; there is no WLC or CAPWAP tunnel to troubleshoot in this architecture.

best_practices: Use DNA Center as the single management plane when the deployment mixes wired SDA and wireless WLC/AP infrastructure.
security_notes: Deep-dive 802.11 encryption/authentication detail (WPA2/WPA3 mechanics) is explicitly out of scope for this material — flagged as covered in an earlier volume.
interview_questions: Why doesn't a SOHO Access Point use CAPWAP?
What is the architectural difference between an Autonomous AP and a Lightweight AP?
What role does DNA Center play in a converged wired/wireless network?
exam_traps: Assuming every AP uses CAPWAP — only lightweight APs paired with a WLC do; Autonomous APs never do.
enterprise_notes: Cisco SDA's converged wired+wireless management (via DNA Center) is positioned as the modern enterprise campus design versus standalone SOHO gear.
